'''James Graham''' (born 10 September 1985) is an English former professional [[rugby league]] player who played as a {{rlp|pr}} for [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] in the [[Super League]], and at international level for [[England national rugby league team|England]] and [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]].

After 224 appearances for St Helens,<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/player/76 |title=Player Profile - James Graham |website=St Helens R.F.C |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101129063158/http://saintsrlfc.com/player/17 |archive-date=2010-11-29 |access-date=2011-06-01}}</ref> and having won a number of Championships and [[Challenge Cup]]s with them, he moved to Sydney for the [[2012 NRL season]]. Graham has played for the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] and the [[St. George Illawarra Dragons]] in the [[National Rugby League]], and has also played for the [[NRL All Stars team|NRL All Stars]].

Graham lost six grand finals in a row, including five in a row with [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] in the [[Super League]] competition across the 2007–2011 seasons, and with the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] in the Australian [[National Rugby League]] in 2012.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Proszenko |first=Adrian |url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/canterbury-bulldogs-dally-mwinning-prop-james-graham-eager-to-break-finals-losing-streak-20140930-10o4tp.html |title=Canterbury Bulldogs' Dally M-winning prop James Graham eager to break finals losing streak |date=2014-09-30 |work=The Sydney Morning Herald|access-date=2018-07-20 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180720165617/https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/canterbury-bulldogs-dally-mwinning-prop-james-graham-eager-to-break-finals-losing-streak-20140930-10o4tp.html |archive-date=2018-07-20 |publisher=Fairfax Media |url-access=limited}}</ref> He then played in the [[2014 NRL Grand Final]] defeat by the [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]], and he also featured for England in the [[2017 Rugby League World Cup]] Final defeat by Australia.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Pengilly |first=Adam |url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/rugby-league-world-cup-2017-englands-james-graham-wont-apologise-for-sledging-20171202-gzxim1.html |title=Rugby League World Cup 2017: England's James Graham won't apologise for sledging |date=2017-12-02 |work=The Sydney Morning Herald|access-date=2017-07-20 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180720140004/https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/rugby-league-world-cup-2017-englands-james-graham-wont-apologise-for-sledging-20171202-gzxim1.html |archive-date=2018-07-20 |url-access=limited}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Lane |first=Daniel final memory he'll cherish forever |date=2014-10-11 |work=The Sydney Morning Herald|access-date=2018-07-20 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180720140038/https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/how-james-graham-gave-an-11yearold-boy-a-grand-final-memory-hell-cherish-forever-20141011-114q6z.html |archive-date=2018-07-20 |url-access=limited}}</ref>

==Early life==
Graham was born in [[Maghull]], [[Merseyside, England|Merseyside]], and was educated at [[Deyes High School]].<ref>{{cite web |title=James in a league of his own |url=https://www.liverpoolecho.co.uk/news/liverpool-news/james-league-3518639 |website=Liverpool Echo |access-date=7 January 2026 |date=10 May 2006}}</ref> Graham chose to play [[rugby league]] at an early age after being introduced to the game by his father, John, who originated from [[Cumbria]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Maryport dad inspired Challenge Cup player |url=https://www.timesandstar.co.uk/sport/17046992.maryport-dad-inspired-challenge-cup-player/ |website=Times and Star |access-date=7 January 2026 |date=28 August 2008}}</ref> He began playing junior rugby league for [[Thatto Heath Crusaders|St Helens Crusaders]], and also went on to play for [[Blackbrook A.R.L.F.C.|Blackbrook]] as a teenager.<ref>{{cite web |title=James Graham, St Helens |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2006/aug/08/rugbyleague.sport1 |website=The Guardian |date=8 August 2006}}</ref>

==Club career==
===St Helens===
Graham signed with Saints as a junior in 2000, eventually playing his way through the Junior Academies before making his senior début against [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]] in August 2003. Graham already had leadership experience after captaining the England Academy in a famous series victory in Australia in 2004. {{citation needed|date=September 2014}}

Graham played for St Helens from the interchange bench in their [[2006 Challenge Cup]] Final victory against [[Huddersfield Giants|Huddersfield]]. Then-[[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] coach [[Brian Noble (rugby league)|Brian Noble]] selected Graham in a friendly against [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] earlier in 2006 in the [[Castlemaine XXXX]] Test in which he made a try scoring début at [[Knowsley Road]]. St Helens reached the [[2006 Super League Grand final]] to be contested against [[Hull F.C.|Hull FC]], and Graham played from the interchange bench in Saints' 26–4 victory. As 2006 Super League champions, St Helens faced 2006 NRL Premiers the [[Brisbane Broncos]] in the [[2007 World Club Challenge]]. Graham played from the substitute bench in Saints' 18–14 victory.

The young prop made a name for himself in 2007's [[Super League XII]], making 27 first team appearances and being named Saints' Young Player of the Year {{citation needed|date=October 2015}} which has also seen him named as a contender for Super League Young Player of the Year, with competition from [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] rival [[Chris Ashton]]. He signed a new contract at [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] to tie his future at the [[Knowsley Road]] based club until 2011. {{citation needed|date=September 2014}}

He was named in the [[Super League Dream Team]] for 2008's [[Super League XIII|2008]] season.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=11755 |title=2008 engage Super League Dream Team |date=2008-09-15 |website=Super League |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100721195910/http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=11755 |archive-date=2010-07-21 |access-date=2008-09-26}}</ref> James won the 2008 [[Man of Steel award]], making him the fourth successive St Helens player to win the prestigious award having beaten rival [[Jamie Peacock]], and teammate [[Leon Pryce]]. He follows teammates [[James Roby]], [[Paul Wellens]], [[Jamie Lyon]], [[Paul Sculthorpe]] (two spells) and [[Sean Long]] in being named Man of Steel.
[[file:James Graham 2011.jpg|thumb|left|200px|Graham playing for St Helens in 2011]]
He was named in the Rugby League Writers' team of the year in 2008 and the ''[[Rugby League World]]'' magazine the following year in 2009. {{citation needed|date=September 2015}}

He played in the [[2009 Super League Grand Final]] defeat by the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Old Trafford]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=Fletcher |first=Paul |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/8290503.stm |title=St Helens 10-18 Leeds Rhinos |date=2009-10-10 |work=BBC Sport |access-date=2019-11-22 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191210213641/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/8290503.stm |archive-date=2019-12-10 |publisher=British Broadcasting Corporation}}</ref>

2011 would be Graham's final year at Saints as a host of [[National Rugby League|NRL]] clubs coveted his signature. [[Canterbury Bulldogs]] and [[Parramatta Eels]] were thought to head the list of interested clubs.{{citation needed|date=March 2020}}

On 27 April 2011 it was confirmed by club officials that [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] joint captain Graham would join [[National Rugby League|NRL]] side the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] when his contract expires at the end of the 2011 season. "We did everything practical to persuade James to stay," said Saints chairman Eamonn McManus. "But we respect his wishes and ambitions. There comes a point where you have to just shake his hand and wish him luck." Graham left St Helens with a 4–5 record in finals (1-5 in [[Super League Grand Final]]s in 2006, and 2007–2011; and 3–0 in [[Challenge Cup]] Finals from 2006 to 2008).<ref>{{Cite news |last=Proszenko |first=Adrian |url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/canterbury-bulldogs/canterbury-bulldogs-dally-mwinning-prop-james-graham-eager-to-break-finals-losing-streak-20140930-10o4tp.html |title=Canterbury Bulldogs' Dally M-winning prop James Graham eager to break finals losing streak |date=2014-09-30 |work=The Sydney Morning Herald |access-date=2014-10-25 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141002085416/http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/canterbury-bulldogs/canterbury-bulldogs-dally-mwinning-prop-james-graham-eager-to-break-finals-losing-streak-20140930-10o4tp.html |archive-date=2014-10-02 |publisher=Fairfax Media |url-access=limited}}</ref>

He played in the [[2011 Super League Grand Final]] defeat by the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Old Trafford]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=Wilson |first=Andy |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/oct/08/super-league-grand-final-match-report?newsfeed=true |title=Leeds claim Grand Final glory as inspired Rob Burrow sinks St Helens |date=2011-10-08 |work=The Guardian |access-date=2019-11-21 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170308150440/https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/oct/08/super-league-grand-final-match-report?newsfeed=true |archive-date=2017-03-08}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Stott |first=Julie |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/8815944/St-Helens-16-Leeds-32-match-report.html |title=St Helens 16 Leeds 32: match report |date=2011-10-08 |work=The Telegraph |access-date=2019-11-21 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170308142015/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/8815944/St-Helens-16-Leeds-32-match-report.html |archive-date=2017-03-08}}</ref>

===Canterbury-Bankstown===
He played 26 games in his début NRL season and was part of the Canterbury team which lost the [[2012 NRL Grand Final|Grand Final]] to [[Melbourne Storm]]. However he was at the centre of the match's most controversial incident when he appeared to bite the left ear of Storm fullback [[Billy Slater]]. Graham denied the charge but despite inconclusive video footage<ref>{{Cite news |last=Webster |first=Andrew |url=http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/graham-judiciary/story-e6frfgbo-1226488427893?nk=c38f8e155c54da3c83cbd9b0d8cea027 |title=James Graham cops 12 match ban for biting Billy Slater's ear during the 2012 grand final |date=2012-10-05 |work=Herald Sun |access-date=2014-07-13 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200322001942/https://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/graham-judiciary/news-story/ced4fd67fe771e1b0fd804282522372e |archive-date=2020-03-22 |publisher=News Limited}}</ref> was widely condemned and subsequently suspended for 12 matches by the [[NRL Judiciary]] in a hearing lasting ten minutes.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Webster |first=Andrew |url=http://www.news.com.au/sport/nrl/bulldog-james-graham-insists-he-didnt-bite-billy-slater/story-fndujljl-1226489479242 |title=Bulldog James Graham insists he didn't bite Billy Slater |date=2012-10-06 |work=The Daily Telegraph |access-date=2012-10-10 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121008002345/http://www.news.com.au/sport/nrl/bulldog-james-graham-insists-he-didnt-bite-billy-slater/story-fndujljl-1226489479242 |archive-date=8 October 2012 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Jackson |first=Glenn |url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/im-a-lover-not-a-biter-defiant-graham-maintains-innocence-20121004-271tw.html |title=I'm a lover not a biter; defiant Graham maintains innocence |date=2012-10-04 |work=The Sydney Morning Herald|access-date=2012-10-10 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121007021746/http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/im-a-lover-not-a-biter-defiant-graham-maintains-innocence-20121004-271tw.html |archive-date=2012-10-07 |publisher=Fairfax Media |url-access=limited}}</ref>
[[File:James Graham's 400th game.jpg|thumb|Graham after his 400th game]]
After Canterbury captain [[Michael Ennis]] was ruled out of the [[2014 NRL Grand Final]] due to a foot injury, Graham along with teammate [[Trent Hodkinson]] were named co-captains of the Bulldogs for the match.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Carayannis |first=Michael |url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/canterbury-bulldogs-captain-michael-ennis-ruled-out-of-nrl-grand-final-james-graham-trent-hodkinson-named-as-cocaptains-20141004-10q8sc.html |title=Canterbury Bulldogs captain Michael Ennis ruled out of NRL grand final, James Graham, Trent Hodkinson named as co-captains |date=2014-10-04 |work=The Sydney Morning Herald|access-date=2016-07-10 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160130192412/http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/canterbury-bulldogs-captain-michael-ennis-ruled-out-of-nrl-grand-final-james-graham-trent-hodkinson-named-as-cocaptains-20141004-10q8sc.html |archive-date=2016-01-30 |publisher=Fairfax Media |url-access=limited}}</ref> At the Bulldogs 2015 season launch, Graham was named the club captain for the club's 80th season, replacing former rake [[Michael Ennis]]. It was also announced that the Englishman will be joined in the role with newly appointed vice-captains, [[Aiden Tolman]] and [[Frank Pritchard]].

===St. George Illawarra Dragons===
On 5 September during NRL 360, Graham announced that he had signed a three- year deal with [[St. George Illawarra]] starting in the [[2018 NRL season]]. The deal was confirmed by his previous club Canterbury-Bankstown and future club St. George Illawarra on 7 September 2017.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/41161543 |title=James Graham: England prop to join St George Illawarra Dragons |date=2017-09-05 |work=BBC Sport |access-date=2017-10-18 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170906101603/https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/41161543 |archive-date=2017-09-06 |publisher=British Broadcasting Corporation |language=en-GB}}</ref>

In Graham's first year at the Dragons, the club qualified for the finals and defeated Brisbane in week one 48–18 before being eliminated the following week by South Sydney losing 13–12.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Whaley |first=Pamela |url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/live-nrl-south-sydney-rabbitohs-v-st-george-illawarra-dragons-live-score-updates-news-blog/news-story/434579d0fdbe938577ce91970d402046 |title=NRL finals 2018: Rabbitohs defeat Dragons with Adam Reynolds kicking three field goals |date=2018-09-15 |work=Fox Sports Australia |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180916140508/https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/live-nrl-south-sydney-rabbitohs-v-st-george-illawarra-dragons-live-score-updates-news-blog/news-story/434579d0fdbe938577ce91970d402046 |archive-date=2018-09-16 |language=en-AU}}</ref>

Graham played in the club's first 11 games of the [[2019 NRL season]] but was taken from the field during St. George Illawarra's 22–9 loss against Cronulla. Scans later revealed that Graham had suffered a fibula fracture and was ruled out for 8 weeks.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2019/05/27/dragons-suffer-double-blow-with-graham-lafai-sidelined/ |title=Dragons suffer double blow with Graham, Lafai sidelined |date=2019-05-27 |website=NRL.com |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190527120931/https://www.nrl.com/news/2019/05/27/dragons-suffer-double-blow-with-graham-lafai-sidelined/ |archive-date=2019-05-27}}</ref>

In round 21 of the [[2019 NRL season]], Graham played his 400th first grade game as St. George Illawarra defeated the Gold Coast 40–28 at [[Kogarah Oval]].<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.sportingnews.com/au/league/news/nrl-gold-coast-titans-st-george-illawarra-dragons-coach-defence/5xhsb3rxkpuc1wnerzm4lpu98/ |title=Dragons vs Titans: Gold Coast Titans defence slammed as 'too easy for first grade' |last=McKirdy |first=Lachlan |date=2019-08-10 |website=Sporting News |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190812093454/https://www.sportingnews.com/au/league/news/nrl-gold-coast-titans-st-george-illawarra-dragons-coach-defence/5xhsb3rxkpuc1wnerzm4lpu98/ |archive-date=2019-08-12}}</ref>{{Failed verification|date=March 2020}}<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/teams/dragons/once-in-a-generation-graham-to-celebrate-400th-game/news-story/1350b2c57ffec80ad7036c99ad55f8b3/ |title='Once in a generation' Graham to celebrate 400th game |date=2019-08-09 |work=Fox Sports |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190815144433/https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/teams/dragons/once-in-a-generation-graham-to-celebrate-400th-game/news-story/1350b2c57ffec80ad7036c99ad55f8b3 |archive-date=2019-08-15 |agency=AAP}}</ref>

Graham made a total of 19 appearances for St. George Illawarra in the [[2019 NRL season]] as the club endured one of their worst ever seasons finishing 15th on the table.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.sportingnews.com/au/league/news/paul-mcgregor-facing-the-axe-as-st-george-illawarra-dragons-coach-after-horror-season/1m4wnmg28k3j1mhmkd7qlvj2x/ |title=Paul McGregor facing the axe as St. George Illawarra Dragons coach following horror season |last=Chisholm |first=Ed |date=2019-09-02 |website=Sporting News |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190905113456/https://www.sportingnews.com/au/league/news/paul-mcgregor-facing-the-axe-as-st-george-illawarra-dragons-coach-after-horror-season/1m4wnmg28k3j1mhmkd7qlvj2x/ |archive-date=2019-09-05}}</ref>

===St Helens===
On 25 June 2020, Graham was released by St. George to pursue an opportunity to go back home to St Helens.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2020-06-25|title=Dragons officially release James Graham|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2020/06/25/dragons-officially-release-james-graham/|access-date=2020-06-25|website=National Rugby League|language=en}}</ref>

Graham played in the [[2020 Super League Grand Final]] which St Helens won 8–4 in dramatic circumstances at the [[KCOM Stadium]] in Hull. It was Graham's final game as a player.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/55072997|title=Super League Grand Final: Wigan 4-8 St Helens|work=BBC Sport }}</ref>

==Representative career==
[[File:James Graham England.jpg|thumb|right|Graham representing England in 2011]]
[[File:James Graham England 2016.jpg|thumb|right|Graham warming up for England in 2016]]
James made his international début for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] in the 2006, scoring twice in a mid-season international with New Zealand. He missed the Tri-Nations later that season after injuring himself during a drunken episode involving his St Helens teammates. He went on to make another three Great Britain appearances which would all come in the [[2007 All Golds Tour]] before the nation would be ceased and split up into three countries: [[England national rugby league team|England]], [[Scotland national rugby league team|Scotland]] and [[Wales national rugby league team|Wales]].

The following year in June, James made his début for the re-established [[England national rugby league team|England team]] against [[France national rugby league team|France]] in Toulouse. James would go on to play for England in one more fixture, against Wales in Doncaster, before being selected in the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]] team.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/7656553.stm |title=Purdham earns World Cup call-up |date=2008-10-07 |work=BBC Sport |access-date=2008-10-07 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081009081658/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/7656553.stm |archive-date=2008-10-09 |publisher=British Broadcasting Corporation}}</ref> He would go on to appear in 3 of England's 4 appearances in their campaign.

He was selected to play for England against France in the one-off test in 2010.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Hadfield |first=Dave |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/widdop-passes-his-first-test-at-a-canter-1999087.html |title=Widdop passes his first Test at a canter |date=2010-06-13 |work=The Independent |access-date=2010-07-08 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304054922/https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/widdop-passes-his-first-test-at-a-canter-1999087.html |archive-date=2016-03-04 |publisher=Independent Print Limited |location=UK}}</ref>

Due to an injury to tour [[Captain (sports)|captain]] [[Adrian Morley]], Graham was handed over the captaincy of England's [[2010 Four Nations]] tournament squad, and in doing so became one of the youngest ever captains of the national side.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Hadfield |first=Dave |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/i-didnt-have-a-clue-what-league-was-says-new-england-captain-2113154.html |title='I didn't have a clue what league was,' says new England captain |date=2010-10-22 |work=[[The Independent]] |access-date=2010-10-23 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101023064643/https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/i-didnt-have-a-clue-what-league-was-says-new-england-captain-2113154.html |archive-date=2010-10-23 |location=UK}}</ref>
Graham played in the [[2011 Rugby League Four Nations|2011 Four Nations]] and [[2013 Rugby League World Cup]].

Graham was the vice-captain of England at the [[2014 Rugby League Four Nations|2014 Four Nations]]. He captained the team in their match against [[Samoa national rugby league team|Samoa]].

At the conclusion of the 2015 domestic season, Graham was selected to play for England in the [[2015 New Zealand rugby league tour of Great Britain|2015 end-of-year internationals]] against France and New Zealand.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.englandrl.co.uk/article/47305/mcnamara-names-24-man-squad-for |title=McNamara names 24-man squad for 2015 International Series |date=2015-10-11 |website=England Rugby League |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151117031042/http://www.englandrl.co.uk/article/47305/mcnamara-names-24-man-squad-for |archive-date=2015-11-17 |access-date=2015-10-11}}</ref> He appeared in the France [http://www.sportinglife.com/rugby-league/live/match-report/43791/england-v-france test match] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151026223631/http://www.sportinglife.com/rugby-league/live/match-report/43791/england-v-france |date=26 October 2015 }} where England went on to rout their opponents.<ref>{{Cite news |url=http://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/live/match/43791/report |title=England demolish France 84-4 in record win |date=2015-10-24 |work=Sky Sports |access-date=2015-10-24 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151031044932/http://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/live/match/43791/report |archive-date=2015-10-31 |publisher=Sky UK}}</ref> He made history in the opening test-match of the series against New Zealand as he officially became the most capped England international, making his 28th appearance for England, since their re-inauguration from [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]]. He expressed his pride and joy after realising he had now officially beaten [[Kevin Sinfield]]'s record.<ref>{{Cite news |url=http://www.sbs.com.au/news/article/2015/11/05/grahams-joy-england-rl-cap-record |title=Graham's joy at England RL cap record |date=2015-11-05 |work=SBS News |access-date=2015-11-05 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170301181857/http://www.sbs.com.au/news/article/2015/11/05/grahams-joy-england-rl-cap-record |archive-date=2017-03-01 |publisher=Special Broadcasting Service |agency=AAP}}</ref>

In October 2016, Graham was selected in the England squad for the [[2016 Rugby League Four Nations|2016 Four Nations]]. Before the tournament, he captained the team in a [[2016 Rugby League Four Nations#France vs England|test match]] against France in the absence of 'usual' captain [[Sam Burgess]] after he was unavailable due to suspension. Graham captained England to a 40–6 victory.

In October 2017 he was selected in the [[2017 Rugby League World Cup squads#England|England squad]] for the [[2017 Rugby League World Cup]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=Walker |first=Gareth |url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/james-roby-back-fold-wayne-11312505 |title=England rugby league World Cup squad revealed - and James Roby is back in the fold |date=2017-10-09 |work=The Mirror |access-date=2019-10-30 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191030131008/https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/james-roby-back-fold-wayne-11312505 |archive-date=2019-10-30}}</ref> England made it to the final but were defeated by Australia 6–0.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Davidson |first=John |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/rugby-league-world-cup-final-2017-england-vs-australia-highlights-result-scoe-a8088241.html |title=Rugby League World Cup final: England suffer heartbreaking 6-0 defeat as Australia retain title |date=2017-12-02 |work=The Independent |access-date=2018-07-20 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180705071326/https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/rugby-league-world-cup-final-2017-england-vs-australia-highlights-result-scoe-a8088241.html |archive-date=2018-07-05 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

In 2018 he was selected for [[England national rugby league team|England]] against [[France national rugby league team|France]] at the [[Leigh Sports Village]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=Jolly |first=Richard |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/rugby-league/2018/10/17/tom-johnstone-marks-debut-england-hat-trick-against-france/ |title=Tom Johnstone marks debut with England hat-trick against France |date=2018-10-17 |work=The Telegraph |access-date=2019-10-31 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191031085058/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/rugby-league/2018/10/17/tom-johnstone-marks-debut-england-hat-trick-against-france/ |archive-date=2019-10-31}}</ref>

On 9 October, Graham was selected as captain of the England [[2019 Rugby League World Cup 9s]] Squad.<ref name="Sam Tomkins eager to lift inaugural World 9s title with England Down Under">{{Cite news |last=Walker |first=Gareth |url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/sam-tomkins-eager-lift-inaugural-20595184 |title=Sam Tomkins eager to lift inaugural World 9s title with England Down Under |date=2019-10-16 |work=The Mirror |access-date=2019-10-29 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191029125436/https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/sam-tomkins-eager-lift-inaugural-20595184 |archive-date=2019-10-29}}</ref>

He was selected in the squad for the [[2019 Great Britain Lions tour]] of the Southern Hemisphere.<ref name="Zak Hardaker shock inclusion in 24-man Great Britain squad for tour of New Zealand and Papua New Guinea">{{Cite news |last=Heppenstall |first=Ross |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/rugby-league/2019/10/14/zak-hardaker-shock-inclusion-24-man-great-britain-squad-tour/ |title=Zak Hardaker shock inclusion in 24-man Great Britain squad for tour of New Zealand and Papua New Guinea |date=2019-10-14 |work=The Telegraph |access-date=2019-10-29 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191015114140/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/rugby-league/2019/10/14/zak-hardaker-shock-inclusion-24-man-great-britain-squad-tour/ |archive-date=2019-10-15}}</ref>

Graham announced his international retirement in September 2020.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rugby-league.com/article/57135/james-graham-confirms-retirement-from-international-rugby-league#:~:text=But%20following%20the%20cancellation%20of,a%20close%20his%20representative%20career.|title=JAMES GRAHAM CONFIRMS RETIREMENT FROM INTERNATIONAL RUGBY LEAGUE|website=Rugby-League.com|access-date=5 October 2020}}</ref>

== Post playing ==
Following retirement, Graham works as rugby league commentator for [[Triple M Sydney]] and also runs the Bye Round Podcast. In 2021, Graham returned to St. George Illawarra and he took up a role with the clubs NRLW squad.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-01-22 |title=James Graham returns to St George Illawarra Dragons in off-field role |url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/james-graham-returns-to-st-george-illawarra-dragons-in-off-field-role |access-date=2025-04-08 |website=Love Rugby League |language=en}}</ref>

Graham appeared in 2024 ABC series [[Plum (TV series)|''Plum'']].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Walker |first=Callum |title=Ex-St Helens stalwart James Graham stars in Australian TV drama – Total Rugby League |url=https://www.totalrl.com/ex-st-helens-stalwart-james-graham-stars-in-australian-tv-drama/ |access-date=2025-04-08 |language=en-US}}</ref>
On 18 February 2025, Graham was inducted with a life membership for the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-02-18 |title=90th AGM Concluded: Graham and Smith Bestowed Life Membership |url=https://www.bulldogs.com.au/news/2025/02/18/90th-agm-concluded-graham-and-smith-bestowed-life-membership/ |access-date=2025-04-08 |website=Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs |language=en}}</ref>
Following Canterbury's 2025 elimination semi-final loss to Penrith, Graham came under criticism after he called referee Ashley Klein a “bottless git” and an “absolute bottle job” live on air while calling the game with the Triple M radio team.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.news.com.au/sport/nrl/nrl-legend-james-graham-launches-ugly-tirade-at-referee-warned-of-being-pulled-off-air/news-story/8d148232ead8c1f7e418515fff46008b/|title=NRL legend James Graham launches ugly tirade at referee, warned of being pulled off air|website=www.news.com.au}}</ref>
